title: Gini Impurity
description: Learn how Gini impurity measures class mixing in a node.
takeaways: Gini impurity measures how mixed the classes are in a node.
A pure node has Gini impurity of 0.
Maximum impurity for two classes occurs when classes are evenly split.

practiceLab.objective: Compute class proportions and Gini impurity, then compare pure, mixed, and evenly mixed nodes.
instructions: Run the imports cell and inspect the starter labels array (six 0s and four 1s).
Run the guided proportions cell and confirm p0 and p1 print as 0.60 and 0.40.
Run the guided Gini cell and confirm Gini impurity prints as 0.480.
Run Exercise 1 to compute Gini for a pure node of ten 0s.
Run Exercise 2 so gini_impurity compares nodes A (6/4), B (pure), and C (5/5).
Verify the printed Gini values are about 0.480, 0.000, and 0.500.
Answer the reflection questions about purity and which lesson introduces weighted Gini.
hints: Gini = 1 - sum of squared class proportions.
A pure node must contain only one unique label.
practiceLab.expectedOutput: Printed proportions for the 6/4 node, Gini 0.480, pure-node Gini 0.000, and a three-node comparison including Gini 0.500 for the even split.
